response:
**Results**

We evaluated ten experimental variants of our proposed method on the validation set, reporting mean validation accuracy and standard deviation over five random seeds for each configuration (Table 1). Variant_03 serves as our baseline and omits the proposed component, achieving a validation accuracy of 67.8% ± 0.6. Among the nine variants incorporating the proposed method, only variant_08 surpasses the baseline, attaining a validation accuracy of 70.4% ± 0.5—a substantial absolute improvement of +2.6 percentage points. All other variants underperform relative to the baseline.

Specifically, variant_01 achieved 67.2% ± 0.8, and variant_02 achieved 66.9% ± 0.7, both slightly below baseline performance. Variants_04 (65.1% ± 0.9), _05 (66.3% ± 1.0), _06 (64.7% ± 0.7), _07 (65.8% ± 0.8), _09 (66.5% ± 0.7), and _10 (65.9% ± 0.8) also failed to exceed the baseline, with accuracies ranging from 64.7% to 66.5%. The relatively consistent standard deviations across variants (0.5–1.0) suggest that observed differences are not due to high variability between runs.

These results indicate that, while most configurations of the proposed method do not enhance validation accuracy compared to the baseline, variant_08 provides a notable and consistent improvement. This highlights the sensitivity of our approach to specific hyperparameter choices or architectural configurations. In summary, the proposed component offers tangible benefits only in a particular setting (variant_08), underscoring the importance of careful variant selection and further motivating investigation into the factors contributing to its superior performance.
